questions.question: At common law, the doctrine of operative mistake is generally:

options.A: Broadly construed to protect any party who enters a contract under a misapprehension
options.B: Narrowly construed — the categories of operative mistake are limited and the courts are reluctant to allow mistake to undermine certainty of contract
options.C: Available whenever a party makes an error of judgment about the value of the subject matter
options.D: Confined to mistakes about the identity of the other contracting party

questions.explanation: The law of mistake is one of the most restrictive areas of contract law. The courts approach the doctrine narrowly because allowing mistake to operate too readily would undermine contractual certainty. As a general rule, a party is bound by their apparent agreement even if they subjectively intended something different. Only certain, well-defined categories of mistake — common mistake as to fundamental matters, mutual mistake, or operative unilateral mistake — can render a contract void or subject to equitable relief.

questions.question: A common mistake occurs where:

options.A: The two parties share the same false assumption about a fundamental matter that goes to the root of the contract
options.B: One party is mistaken about the terms of the contract and the other is not
options.C: Both parties make different mistakes about different matters that together render the contract unworkable
options.D: A party mistakenly believes the other party is creditworthy when they are not

questions.explanation: Common mistake arises where both parties to the contract share the same mistaken assumption about a fundamental matter that exists at the time of contracting. Because both parties are equally mistaken, there is no false statement by one party to the other — the mistake is 'common' to them. The most established category of common mistake at common law is res extincta — where both parties are mistaken as to the existence of the subject matter of the contract.

questions.question: In Couturier v Hastie [1856] (HL), a contract was made for the sale of a cargo of corn that had, unknown to either party, already been sold and disposed of before the contract was formed. The House of Lords held:

options.A: The seller was liable in breach of contract for failure to deliver
options.B: The buyer was bound to pay the price because the risk of the cargo's loss had passed to them
options.C: The contract was void as there was no subject matter in existence at the time of contracting
options.D: The contract was voidable and could be rescinded by either party

questions.explanation: In Couturier v Hastie [1856], the House of Lords held that the contract was void because the corn did not exist at the time the contract was made. This is the foundational res extincta authority: a contract for the sale of something that does not exist at the time of contracting is void for common mistake. The principle is codified in the Sale of Goods Act 1893, s6 (and its Irish equivalent under SGSSA 1980), which provides that where goods have perished before a contract is made, the contract is void.

questions.question: In Bell v Lever Brothers Ltd [1932] (HL), the House of Lords held that a common mistake would only render a contract void at common law if:

options.A: The mistake was about the quality of the subject matter and significantly reduced its value
options.B: The mistake was so fundamental as to make the contract essentially and radically different from what the parties had contemplated
options.C: Both parties would have refused to enter the contract had they known the true state of affairs
options.D: The mistake related to the existence of the subject matter, not its quality or characteristics

questions.explanation: Bell v Lever Bros [1932] is the leading authority on common mistake at common law. The House of Lords held, by majority, that common mistake will only void a contract if the mistake is so fundamental that it makes the subject matter of the contract essentially and radically different from what the parties contemplated. A mere mistake as to quality (even a significant one) will not suffice. The high threshold reflects the law's commitment to contractual certainty — the directors' service agreements in Bell were not void even though both parties were unaware of the directors' prior breach.

questions.question: In Great Peace Shipping Ltd v Tsavliris Salvage (International) Ltd [2002] (CA England), the Court of Appeal considered whether equity could provide relief for common mistake where the contract was not void at common law. The Court held:

options.A: Equity has a broad discretion to grant relief for common mistake even where the contract is not void at common law
options.B: There is no separate equitable doctrine of common mistake — if a mistake is not sufficiently fundamental to void the contract at law, equity cannot set it aside either
options.C: Equity can rescind a contract for common mistake where the mistake is about quality rather than existence
options.D: Equity can grant relief for common mistake only where rescission is sought within 12 months of contracting

questions.explanation: In Great Peace Shipping [2002], the Court of Appeal overruled Solle v Butcher [1950] and held that there is no separate equitable jurisdiction to rescind a contract for common mistake where the mistake falls short of the Bell v Lever Bros threshold for voidness at common law. The two-tier system of legal and equitable mistake was rejected: if the mistake is not sufficiently fundamental to void the contract at law, equity cannot be used to achieve the same result through rescission. This position clarifies and restricts the doctrine significantly.

questions.question: Mutual mistake arises where:

options.A: Both parties make the same mistake about a fundamental fact
options.B: The parties are at cross-purposes — each is mistaken but about different things, and they are talking past each other
options.C: One party is mistaken and the other is aware of, but does not correct, the mistake
options.D: A party makes a mistake about the effect of a written term they have signed

questions.explanation: Mutual mistake (sometimes called 'cross-purposes' mistake) occurs where the parties are at cross-purposes — each has a different understanding of what the contract is about, but each is mistaken in a different way. For example, the seller believes they are selling one item, the buyer believes they are buying another. In Raffles v Wichelhaus [1864], both parties referred to a ship 'Peerless' but meant different ships of that name. If the objective test cannot determine which party's understanding should prevail, the contract may be void for mutual mistake.

questions.question: Unilateral mistake occurs where only one party is mistaken. At common law, a contract will be void for unilateral mistake only where:

options.A: The mistaken party was induced into the mistake by their own negligence
options.B: The non-mistaken party knew of the mistake (or was deemed to know), particularly a mistake as to the terms of the contract
options.C: The mistake relates to the price of the subject matter and is significant
options.D: The mistaken party communicated their mistake to the other party before signing

questions.explanation: Unilateral mistake will only operate to void a contract at common law in very limited circumstances. The key category is where the non-mistaken party knew of the other's mistake — particularly a mistake about the fundamental terms of the contract (e.g., the price). In such cases, the non-mistaken party cannot 'snatch at a bargain' they know the other party never intended to offer. The court will not allow the non-mistaken party to enforce a contract based on an agreement they knew the other party did not intend to make.

questions.question: Mistake as to identity — where one party is mistaken about the identity of the person they are contracting with — is most likely to void a contract where:

options.A: The contract is made in writing and the rogue uses a false name on the document
options.B: The contract is made inter absentes (not face to face), and identity is fundamental to the decision to contract
options.C: The goods are of high value and the identity of the buyer affects their creditworthiness
options.D: The rogue has committed a criminal offence in obtaining the contract

questions.explanation: The law distinguishes between identity mistakes made face-to-face and those made inter absentes. Where parties deal face to face, the courts presume that the seller intends to deal with the person physically present — the contract is merely voidable for fraud, not void for mistake, meaning a bona fide purchaser for value without notice can obtain good title (Phillips v Brooks [1919], Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003]). Where the contract is made at a distance (e.g., by letter), identity is more likely to be treated as fundamental, making the contract void ab initio: Cundy v Lindsay [1878].

questions.question: In Shogun Finance Ltd v Hudson [2003] (HL), the House of Lords addressed mistake as to identity in the context of a hire-purchase transaction. The majority held:

options.A: The contract between the finance company and the rogue was voidable, not void, for mistake as to identity
options.B: The contract was void ab initio because the finance company intended to deal with the named individual (whose identity had been stolen) not the rogue who was physically present
options.C: Mistake as to identity can never void a consumer transaction
options.D: The rogue's victim had no remedy against the finance company because the transaction was conducted at arm's length

questions.explanation: In Shogun Finance v Hudson [2003], the House of Lords (by majority) held that where the contract was made in writing using a stolen identity document, the finance company contracted with the person named in the document, not the rogue. Since the named person was not a party, the contract was void for mistake as to identity. Title could not pass to the rogue and therefore could not pass to Hudson (the innocent third party purchaser). The dissent argued that the transaction was merely voidable and that the innocent purchaser should be protected.

questions.question: The non est factum doctrine ('it is not my deed') allows a party to avoid a signed contract where:

options.A: The party signed the contract without reading it and the terms are unexpectedly onerous
options.B: The document signed is fundamentally different in character from what the party believed they were signing, and the party was not negligent in signing
options.C: The party signed under duress and the signature was therefore not voluntary
options.D: The party misunderstood the legal effect of a clause in the contract

questions.explanation: Non est factum is a narrow doctrine allowing a party to escape a signed document where: (1) the document is fundamentally or radically different in character from what they believed they were signing (not merely different in detail); and (2) they were not negligent in signing. In Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] (HL), the House of Lords restricted the doctrine — the document must differ fundamentally in character (e.g., signing a gift when you believe it is a mortgage) not merely in terms. Careless failure to read the document defeats the plea.

questions.question: Where parties enter a written contract that does not accurately record what they actually agreed, the appropriate remedy is:

options.A: Rescission of the contract as void for mistake
options.B: Rectification — an equitable remedy that corrects the written document to reflect the true agreement
options.C: Specific performance of the terms as written in the contract
options.D: A declaration that the written terms are unenforceable

questions.explanation: Rectification is the equitable remedy that corrects a written contract where it fails to accurately record the parties' actual prior agreement. It is not a remedy for mistake about the subject matter of the contract — rather, it corrects a mistake in the recording of an agreement. Requirements: (1) a prior agreement (common continuing intention); (2) which is not reflected in the written instrument; (3) and the court will rewrite the document to accord with the true prior agreement. Joscelyne v Nissen [1970] is the leading English authority; the remedy is also available in Irish equity.

questions.question: In the context of rectification, 'common continuing intention' means:

options.A: Both parties must have had identical thoughts at every stage of negotiations
options.B: There must have been an outward expression of accord between the parties on a specific point which was then erroneously omitted or misstated in the written contract
options.C: The parties must have entered a binding oral contract before the written agreement was drafted
options.D: The parties must have agreed the error was made and jointly applied for rectification

questions.explanation: In Joscelyne v Nissen [1970], the Court of Appeal held that rectification requires proof of a 'common continuing intention' — an outward expression of accord (not necessarily a prior binding contract) on the specific term that was incorrectly recorded. The prior accord must have continued up to the time of executing the written document. Subjective uncommunicated intentions are insufficient — there must be an objectively ascertainable agreement that the written contract fails to capture.

questions.question: Where a contract is void for mistake at common law, what is the effect on title to goods that have been transferred under the contract?

options.A: Title passes to the purchaser because delivery was voluntary
options.B: No title passes — nemo dat quod non habet — and the original owner can recover the goods from any third party including a bona fide purchaser for value
options.C: Title passes if a bona fide third party purchaser has paid a reasonable price
options.D: Title passes to the purchaser subject to a charge in favour of the original owner

questions.explanation: If a contract is void ab initio for mistake, no property passes under it. The nemo dat quod non habet (no one gives what they do not have) principle applies — the seller, having no title, cannot pass title to the buyer or to any subsequent purchaser. This has harsh consequences for innocent third parties who purchase in good faith. This is why the distinction between void and voidable contracts is so important in mistake cases involving identity — only voidable contracts allow a bona fide purchaser for value without notice to acquire good title.

questions.question: Res sua mistake refers to a situation where:

options.A: A party enters a contract to buy something they already own
options.B: Both parties are mistaken as to the quality of the goods
options.C: A party sells something they do not have the right to sell
options.D: The subject matter of the contract is destroyed after the contract is concluded

questions.explanation: Res sua (one's own thing) is a category of common mistake alongside res extincta. It arises where a person purports to buy property that they in fact already own — neither party realises that the buyer is already the legal owner. The contract is void because the subject matter in the relevant sense (i.e., as something the seller can transfer) does not exist. The principle was applied in Cooper v Phibbs [1867] (HL) where a person agreed to lease a fishery that they in fact owned — the agreement was set aside in equity.

questions.question: In McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission [1951] (HCA), the High Court of Australia distinguished Couturier v Hastie by holding the contract was not void where:

options.A: The claimant was the party who made the mistake about the subject matter's existence
options.B: The defendant had impliedly warranted that the subject matter existed — the risk of non-existence was allocated to the defendant by the contract itself
options.C: The subject matter had once existed but was destroyed before delivery was due
options.D: Both parties were aware the subject matter might not exist when the contract was formed

questions.explanation: In McRae v Commonwealth Disposals Commission [1951], the High Court of Australia held that the Commission had impliedly warranted the existence of a particular oil tanker at a specified location when it sold salvage rights over it. Because one party had impliedly contracted for the existence of the subject matter, the risk of its non-existence was allocated by the contract to that party — the contract was not void for common mistake, and the Commission was liable for breach of warranty. This illustrates that mistake and contractual risk allocation interact closely.

questions.question: A mistake as to quality of the subject matter will render a contract void at common law:

options.A: Where the quality significantly affects the value of the subject matter
options.B: Where both parties share the same mistaken belief about quality and would not have contracted but for that belief
options.C: Almost never — mistake as to quality is generally not operative at common law following Bell v Lever Bros
options.D: Where the contract is for the sale of goods and statutory implied terms about quality are breached

questions.explanation: Bell v Lever Bros [1932] established that common mistake as to quality — even where the quality fundamentally affects the value or desirability of the subject matter — will not void a contract at common law. The fact that the service contracts in Bell were less valuable than the parties had supposed (because the directors had committed undisclosed breaches that could have justified summary dismissal) was insufficient to void the contracts. Mistake as to quality is therefore generally not operative at common law, leaving parties to rely on other doctrines such as misrepresentation or implied terms.

questions.question: The equitable jurisdiction in mistake (before Great Peace Shipping narrowed it) was said to arise in cases such as Solle v Butcher [1950] (CA). What was the nature of that jurisdiction?

options.A: Equity could award damages where common law mistake applied but the contract was valid
options.B: Equity could rescind a contract for common mistake on terms, even where the mistake fell short of the Bell v Lever Bros common law threshold
options.C: Equity could vary the terms of a contract where common mistake rendered performance unduly burdensome
options.D: Equity could impose a constructive trust where a party was unjustly enriched by a mistake

questions.explanation: In Solle v Butcher [1950], Denning LJ asserted an equitable jurisdiction to rescind a contract for common mistake 'on terms' — even where the mistake was not sufficiently fundamental to void the contract at common law. The court could rescind and impose conditions (e.g., allowing the defendant to re-let at a higher rent). This broader equitable jurisdiction was overruled by the Court of Appeal in Great Peace Shipping [2002], which held that the threshold for equitable and common law relief in common mistake must be the same.

questions.question: In Irish law, which court has jurisdiction to grant rectification of a written contract?

options.A: Only the Circuit Court, which has exclusive jurisdiction over contract disputes
options.B: The High Court, exercising its equitable jurisdiction
options.C: Any court can grant rectification as it is a common law remedy
options.D: Only the Supreme Court of Ireland can order rectification of commercial contracts

questions.explanation: Rectification is an equitable remedy and is therefore granted by courts exercising equitable jurisdiction. In Ireland, the High Court (and, on appeal, the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court) has full equitable jurisdiction. Since the Judicature (Ireland) Act 1877, legal and equitable jurisdiction are fused and can be exercised by the same court, but the remedy of rectification retains its equitable character and the court applies equitable principles in determining whether to grant it.

questions.question: In the context of mistake as to identity, which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: A contract made face-to-face is always void for mistake as to identity where the other party gives a false name
options.B: A contract made face-to-face is presumed to be with the person physically present — it is voidable for misrepresentation (fraud), not void for mistake as to identity
options.C: A contract made by post is voidable for mistake as to identity where a false name is used
options.D: Mistake as to identity has been abolished as a vitiating factor in Irish law

questions.explanation: The authorities distinguish face-to-face transactions from those made at a distance. In face-to-face dealings, the courts apply a strong presumption that the seller intends to deal with the person physically before them — identity of the person is usually not fundamental to the formation of the contract in this context. The result is that the contract is merely voidable for fraud (not void for mistake), meaning a bona fide purchaser for value without notice can acquire good title: Phillips v Brooks [1919], Lewis v Averay [1972]. This is contrasted with the inter absentes situation in Cundy v Lindsay [1878].

questions.question: A party who has entered a void contract for mistake at common law seeks to recover money paid under it. Their claim is likely to be based on:

options.A: Breach of contract — the other party failed to perform
options.B: Unjust enrichment / total failure of consideration — since the contract is void, no consideration was received for the money paid
options.C: The tort of conversion — the money was wrongfully retained
options.D: Promissory estoppel — the other party is estopped from retaining money paid

questions.explanation: Where a contract is void ab initio for mistake, there is no valid contract on which to sue for breach. Money paid under a void contract is recoverable in unjust enrichment (formerly 'quasi-contract') on the basis of total failure of consideration — the payor received nothing in return for their payment because the supposed contract never existed. This was recognised in Kelner v Baxter [1866] and is consistent with the general principle that money paid under a void transaction can be recovered in restitution. The defendant is unjustly enriched at the claimant's expense.
